title: Water Heater Replacement
description: Put the steps for replacing a residential gas water heater in the correct order.

steps.text: Turn off gas supply at shutoff valve and close cold water inlet
Connect a garden hose and drain the old water heater
Disconnect gas line, water connections, and vent pipe
Remove old water heater and clean area
Set new water heater in place (elevated in garage) and level it
Connect cold and hot water piping with dielectric fittings
Install T&P relief valve and discharge pipe to within 6" of floor
Connect gas line with sediment trap and test all joints for leaks
Connect vent pipe, fill tank with water, then light pilot and verify operation

explanation: Always drain before disconnecting, install T&P discharge pipe before gas, and NEVER light the pilot until the tank is full of water. Firing an empty tank will damage the glass lining and void the warranty. Gas connections must be leak-tested before operation per IFGC 406.4.

title: DWV Rough-In for Bathroom
description: Order the steps for roughing in the DWV (drain-waste-vent) system for a bathroom.

steps.text: Review plans for fixture locations and pipe sizing
Cut and install the building drain and main stack
Install closet bend and flange for toilet (12" rough-in)
Run horizontal branch drains for shower and lavatory
Install P-traps at proper height for each fixture
Install vent piping from trap arms up through roof
Pressure test entire DWV system at 5 PSI for 15 minutes

explanation: DWV rough-in works from the main drain outward: establish the main stack first, then branch drains, then traps, and finally vents. Always test the system before closing walls. IPC 312.2 requires a 5 PSI air test or 10-foot head water test.

title: Fixture Trim-Out
description: Order the steps for trimming out (finishing) a bathroom after walls and flooring are complete.

steps.text: Install water closet wax ring and set toilet on closet flange
Secure toilet with closet bolts and connect water supply
Install lavatory faucet and drain body on sink
Mount sink to vanity and connect supply tubes and P-trap
Install shower trim plate, handle, and shower head
Turn on water supply and check all connections for leaks
Test all fixtures for proper operation and drainage

explanation: Trim-out sequence matters: install faucets on sinks BEFORE mounting (much easier), set toilets on wax rings carefully (one shot — rocking breaks the seal), and always test every connection before leaving the job.

title: Water Service Installation
description: Order the steps for installing a new residential water service from the main to the house.

steps.text: Obtain permit and call 811 for utility locates
Excavate trench from main to building (below frost line)
Install corporation stop at water main with wet tap
Run service pipe (Type K copper or approved material) in trench
Install curb stop and curb box at property line
Sleeve pipe through foundation wall and install main shutoff inside
Pressure test service line at 1.5x working pressure for 2 hours
Backfill trench in lifts and restore surface

explanation: Water service installation starts with utility locates (811) — hitting a gas or electric line is life-threatening. The trench must be below frost line to prevent freezing. Always pressure test before backfilling per IPC 312.5.

title: Drain Cleaning with Cable Machine
description: Order the steps for clearing a clogged drain using a drain cable (snake) machine.

steps.text: Identify the clog location and select the nearest cleanout access
Set up drain cable machine near cleanout and select proper cable size
Remove cleanout plug and insert cable into drain slowly
Run machine forward while feeding cable until resistance is felt
Work cable back and forth through the blockage until it breaks free
Retract cable while running machine and clean cable as it comes out
Run water through the drain to verify flow and clear debris

explanation: Always identify the closest access point first to minimize cable length needed. Feed cable slowly — forcing it can kink the cable or damage the pipe. Clean the cable during retraction to prevent mess. Wear gloves and eye protection at all times.

title: Copper Pipe Soldering (Sweat Joints)
description: Order the steps for making a soldered copper pipe joint.

steps.text: Cut pipe square using a tubing cutter and ream the inside edge
Clean the pipe end with emery cloth (1" beyond insertion depth)
Clean inside of fitting socket with fitting brush
Apply lead-free flux to pipe end and fitting socket
Assemble joint and rotate fitting to distribute flux evenly
Apply heat to fitting body (not the pipe) with torch
Touch solder to joint — when it melts and wicks in, the joint is ready
Wipe excess solder with a damp rag and allow to cool

explanation: Proper soldering technique is critical: clean surfaces allow capillary action to draw solder into the joint. Heat the fitting (not the pipe) so solder flows toward the heat source. Always use lead-free solder on potable water per IPC 605.11.2.

title: Water Main Tap and Connection
description: Order the steps for making a wet tap on a municipal water main for a new service connection.

steps.text: Verify permits and coordinate with water utility for shutdown or wet tap
Excavate around the water main and expose the pipe
Clean the main pipe surface and install the tapping saddle or sleeve
Install the corporation stop on the tapping saddle
Drill through the main using the tapping machine through the corp stop
Close the corporation stop and remove the tapping machine
Connect service pipe to corporation stop and run to curb stop
Flush and pressure test the new service line

explanation: Wet tapping allows connection to a live water main without shutting down service. The corporation stop must be installed and operational before drilling — it is the only shutoff once the main is tapped. This work often requires a licensed utility contractor.

title: Bathroom Remodel Plumbing
description: Order the steps for replumbing a full bathroom remodel where fixture locations are changing.

steps.text: Shut off water supply and drain the existing system
Demo existing fixtures, piping, and open walls/floor as needed
Install new drain and vent piping to new fixture locations
Install new water supply piping (hot and cold) to new locations
Pressure test drain system (5 PSI air) and supply system (working pressure)
Call for rough-in inspection before closing walls
Close walls, install tile/flooring, then trim out fixtures
Call for final inspection after all fixtures are operational

explanation: The rough-in inspection MUST happen before walls are closed — inspectors need to see all piping, slopes, venting, and test results. Closing walls before inspection means tearing them back open. Two inspections are required: rough and final per IPC 107.

title: Sump Pump Installation
description: Order the steps for installing a new sump pump system in a basement.

steps.text: Excavate sump pit location and install pre-formed sump basin
Route perimeter drain tile into sump pit
Set submersible pump in pit and adjust float switch level
Install check valve on discharge pipe above pump
Run discharge pipe through rim joist to exterior, maintaining slope
Terminate discharge at least 10 feet from foundation with splash block
Install sealed pit cover and connect to GFCI outlet
Fill pit with water and test pump operation and check valve function

explanation: The check valve must be installed on the discharge before running the pipe — it prevents water in the discharge line from flowing back into the pit when the pump shuts off. Without it, the pump cycles constantly. Test by filling with water, never dry-run a pump per IPC 713.

title: Gas Line Pressure Test
description: Order the steps for pressure testing a new gas piping system before placing it in service.

steps.text: Verify all joints are complete and all open ends are capped or plugged
Close the main gas shutoff valve (system isolated from supply)
Connect a test gauge and air/nitrogen source to a test port
Pressurize the system to the required test pressure (typically 3 PSI for low-pressure systems)
Disconnect the pressure source and hold for minimum test duration (varies by code, often 10-30 minutes)
Verify no pressure drop on the test gauge
If pressure holds, apply leak detection solution to every joint for final verification
Document test results, pressure, duration, and submit to inspector

explanation: Gas line pressure testing is a life-safety requirement. The system must hold at test pressure with NO drop. Even a tiny leak can cause a gas explosion. Never use a flame to test for leaks — always use approved leak detection solution or electronic detectors per IFGC 406.4.

title: Water Heater Flush and Maintenance
description: Order the steps for performing annual maintenance on a tank-type gas water heater.

steps.text: Turn off gas supply and set thermostat to pilot or vacation mode
Connect garden hose to drain valve and route to floor drain
Close cold water inlet valve and open drain valve
Open a nearby hot water faucet to break vacuum and allow draining
After draining several gallons, briefly open cold inlet to stir sediment
Close drain valve, remove hose, close the hot faucet
Open cold water inlet and refill tank completely (water runs clear from faucet)
Test T&P relief valve by lifting the lever briefly and verify it reseats
Restore gas supply, relight if needed, and verify proper operation

explanation: Annual flushing removes sediment that reduces efficiency and causes premature tank failure. Opening a hot faucet breaks the vacuum so water actually flows out of the drain valve. Always test the T&P valve during maintenance — it is the primary safety device per IPC 504.6.

title: Backflow Preventer Testing
description: Order the steps for performing an annual test on a Reduced Pressure Zone (RPZ) backflow preventer.

steps.text: Verify the RPZ is accessible, identify test cocks, and record serial number
Install the differential pressure test gauge kit on the test cocks
Close the downstream shutoff valve to test the second check valve
Record the pressure differential across the second check valve (minimum 1 PSI)
Test the relief valve by creating a condition where it must open
Record the relief valve opening point (must open before back pressure equals supply)
Test the first check valve differential pressure (minimum 1 PSI)
Remove test equipment, restore valves to normal operation
Complete test report and submit to water purveyor within required timeframe

explanation: RPZ testing must be performed by a certified backflow tester using calibrated equipment. Testing the second check first is critical — if you test the first check first, you cannot verify the second independently. Results must be reported to the water utility per IPC 312.10 and ASSE 5110.

title: Union Certification: Backflow Tester Certification
description: Order the steps for performing a field test on an RPZ backflow preventer as taught for ASSE 5110 certification at the UA union hall.

steps.text: Close the downstream shutoff valve to isolate the assembly
Install differential pressure test gauge kit on the test cocks
Test check valve #2 by reading the differential pressure across it (minimum 1 PSI)
Test the differential pressure relief valve by verifying it opens before back pressure equals supply pressure
Test check valve #1 by reading the differential pressure across it (minimum 1 PSI)
Record all pressure readings on the certified test form
Open the downstream shutoff valve and restore normal flow
Complete the test report and submit to the water purveyor within the required timeframe

explanation: The ASSE 5110 backflow tester certification is a key UA credential. The test sequence is critical: check valve #2 must be tested first because testing #1 first would equalize pressure and make it impossible to independently verify #2. The relief valve must open before backpressure can reach the supply side. All readings are recorded and submitted to the water purveyor — this protects the public water supply from contamination.

title: Union Training: Cast Iron Soil Pipe Assembly
description: Order the steps for joining traditional cast iron hub-and-spigot soil pipe as taught at the UA union hall.

steps.text: Measure and mark the cast iron pipe to the required length
Cut the pipe using a snap cutter at the marked location
Dry-fit the spigot end into the hub to verify proper alignment and gap
Pack oakum into the hub around the spigot end using a yarning iron
Pour molten lead into the hub joint to fill the remaining depth
Caulk the lead tight against the hub and spigot using a caulking iron
Test the joint for leaks under water pressure
Secure the pipe run with proper hangers at the required intervals

explanation: Cast iron hub-and-spigot joining with molten lead is a traditional plumbing skill still taught at UA halls. Oakum is packed first to create a base seal and prevent lead from entering the pipe bore. Molten lead is poured (never in the rain — moisture causes dangerous splatter) and then caulked for a watertight joint. While no-hub couplings have largely replaced leaded joints, understanding this technique is part of UA journeyman knowledge and is still encountered in repair work on older buildings.

title: Hydronic Expansion Tank Installation
description: Order the steps for sizing and installing a bladder-type expansion tank on a closed-loop hydronic system per Spec 23 21 13.

steps.text: Calculate the total system water volume including piping, coils, and equipment
Size the expansion tank based on system volume, temperature range, and fill pressure
Mount the expansion tank vertically with an isolation valve at the connection point
Connect the tank to the system return piping near the pump suction (point of no pressure change)
Pre-charge the bladder with dry nitrogen to match the system cold fill pressure
Verify the tank pressure gauge reads correctly and matches the pre-charge pressure
Test the system pressure relief valve to ensure it opens at the rated set pressure
Document tank size, pre-charge pressure, system fill pressure, and relief valve set pressure

explanation: Expansion tank installation per Spec 23 21 13 requires proper sizing before selection — an undersized tank causes the relief valve to open during heating. Location at the pump suction (point of no pressure change) is critical per ASHRAE: this ensures pump pressure adds to system pressure everywhere, preventing cavitation and negative pressure zones. Pre-charging with dry nitrogen (not air, which contains moisture) to the cold fill pressure prevents the bladder from being compressed before the system heats up.

title: Copper Pipe Brazing (2.5" and Larger)
description: Order the steps for brazing a large-diameter copper pipe joint (2.5" and above) using sil-fos brazing alloy per ANSI B16.22.

steps.text: Clean the pipe end and fitting socket to bright copper using emery cloth or abrasive pad
Apply brazing flux evenly to the pipe end (not inside the fitting socket)
Assemble the joint ensuring full insertion depth per ANSI B16.22 fitting specifications
Apply heat evenly around the entire joint using an oxy-acetylene torch with a rosebud tip
Feed sil-fos brazing alloy into the joint when the flux becomes clear and liquid
Allow the joint to cool naturally to room temperature — never quench with water
Clean flux residue from the joint exterior with a damp cloth
Pressure test the completed joint at 1.5 times design working pressure

explanation: Brazing large-diameter copper per ANSI B16.22 requires even heat distribution — on 2.5" and larger joints, uneven heating causes the alloy to flow to the hot side and leave voids on the cold side. A rosebud tip provides the wider flame pattern needed. Sil-fos (silver-phosphorus-copper) alloy is self-fluxing on copper-to-copper joints, but flux is still applied for better wetting. Never quench — thermal shock on large joints causes micro-cracks that fail under pressure cycling.

title: Automatic Balancing Valve Installation
description: Order the steps for installing a pressure-independent automatic balancing valve on a hydronic branch circuit per Spec 23 21 13.

steps.text: Verify the pipe size and look up the design flow rate (GPM) from the mechanical drawings
Install the valve in the correct flow direction as indicated by the arrow on the valve body
Install pressure/temperature test ports upstream and downstream of the valve
Set the design flow rate on the valve cartridge using the manufacturer's adjustment tool
Start the system and verify the actual flow reading matches the design GPM
Lock the valve setting with the tamper-proof locking mechanism
Label the valve with the design GPM, circuit served, and date of commissioning
Document the valve setting, measured flow, and differential pressure on the commissioning report

explanation: Automatic balancing valves per Spec 23 21 13 maintain a constant flow rate regardless of system pressure fluctuations — unlike manual balancing valves which drift as other zones open and close. Installing in the correct flow direction is critical; reverse installation causes the valve to shut. P/T test ports allow future flow verification without removing the valve. Locking and labeling prevent unauthorized adjustment that would unbalance the system.

title: Office Breakroom Pantry Sink Installation (high-rise office)
description: Order the steps for installing a Kohler K-3822 pantry sink with Everpure filtration in a high-rise office breakroom per high-rise office specifications.

steps.text: Verify rough-in dimensions and confirm pipe sleeves are installed (Schedule 40 galvanized through masonry, 20 gauge sheet metal through framed walls)
Install Type L copper supply lines through sleeves with maximum 1/2-inch annular space
Install Link-Seal Model OS-316 at breakroom wall penetrations
Mount the Kohler K-3822 pantry sink in countertop and install faucet set to 1.8 GPM default
Connect Everpure 4FC5 water filter to cold water supply under sink
Install 1-1/2" P-trap, trap arm, and connect to waste piping
Install water hammer arresters (J.R. Smith Fig. 5005) on supply lines
Turn on water supply and test all connections for leaks at full system pressure

explanation: Breakroom sink installation in a high-rise starts with verifying sleeves and penetration details — these cannot be corrected after wall finishes are complete. Link-Seals go in before fixtures because access is easier. Water hammer arresters are critical in high-rises where pressure surges travel through the entire riser system. The faucet must default to 1.8 GPM per CPC 2022.

title: Pipe Sleeve Installation Through Masonry Wall (high-rise office)
description: Order the steps for installing a pipe sleeve through a masonry core wall in a high-rise office tenant improvement per high-rise office specifications.

steps.text: Mark the penetration location on the masonry wall per approved shop drawings
Core drill the masonry wall to the sleeve outside diameter
Install Schedule 40 galvanized steel sleeve into the cored opening
Grout the sleeve in place flush with both wall faces
Run the pipe through the sleeve ensuring maximum 1/2-inch annular space
Pack mineral wool tightly into the annular space
Apply Duxseal non-hardening compound on both faces as the finish seal

explanation: Pipe sleeves through masonry walls require Schedule 40 galvanized steel — never sheet metal, which is only for framed partitions. The 1/2-inch maximum annular space ensures the mineral wool and Duxseal can properly seal the penetration. Grouting the sleeve before running the pipe prevents misalignment. This sequence is verified during LEED enhanced commissioning.

title: CRAC Condensate Drain to Lavatory Tailpiece (high-rise office)
description: Order the steps for piping the condensate drain from a CRAC unit in a server room to the lavatory tailpiece per high-rise office specifications.

steps.text: Install the leak detector pad beneath the CRAC unit and wire to solenoid valve control
Connect 3/4-inch condensate drain (CD) to CRAC unit drain pan outlet
Route condensate drain piping with continuous downward slope to the adjacent restroom
Insulate the condensate piping to prevent sweating in the server room environment
Terminate the condensate drain at the lavatory tailpiece with a visible air gap (indirect waste)
Verify the trap primer connection on the server room floor drain is operational
Test the solenoid valve and leak detector interlock by simulating a leak condition

explanation: CRAC condensate must be handled as indirect waste — spilling to a lavatory tailpiece provides the required air gap and makes the discharge visible. The leak detector and solenoid valve interlock must be installed and tested before the CRAC unit is placed in service. Insulating the condensate piping prevents condensation dripping onto server equipment below.

title: High-Rise Firestop Installation at Floor Penetration (high-rise office)
description: Order the steps for firestopping a cast iron soil pipe penetration through a 2-hour rated floor assembly between Levels 30 and 31 per high-rise office specifications.

steps.text: Verify the Schedule 40 galvanized steel sleeve is properly grouted into the floor assembly
Confirm the cast iron pipe with Husky 4000 coupling is centered in the sleeve with maximum 1/2-inch annular space
Pack mineral wool insulation tightly into the annular space to the full depth of the floor assembly
Apply Duxseal non-hardening compound on both the top and bottom faces of the penetration
Verify the firestop assembly matches the listed UL System number for the specific pipe/floor configuration
Affix a firestop inspection tag with UL System number, date, and installer identification
Document the installation with photographs and log it on the firestop tracking spreadsheet for LEED commissioning

explanation: Firestopping in high-rises follows a strict sequence: verify the sleeve, center the pipe, pack mineral wool, seal with Duxseal, confirm the UL System match, tag, and document. The non-hardening Duxseal compound is specified because it remains flexible over the life of the building — hardening compounds crack from thermal cycling and pipe vibration, breaking the fire seal. Every penetration must be documented for LEED enhanced commissioning verification.

title: LEED Enhanced Commissioning — Plumbing Verification (high-rise office)
description: Order the steps for performing LEED enhanced commissioning verification of the plumbing systems across Levels 30-32 of the high-rise commercial office TI.

steps.text: Review all plumbing submittals and shop drawings for specification compliance (pipe materials, fixture models, valve types)
Verify fixture flow rates: lavatories 0.5 GPM, kitchen faucets 1.8 GPM default, urinals 0.125 GPF, water closets 1.28 GPF
Inspect all firestop installations against UL System listings and verify documentation tags are present
Confirm pipe materials: Type L copper above floor, Type K underground, cast iron soil pipe with Husky 4000 couplings
Test all solenoid valves and leak detector interlocks at water heaters and CRAC units
Verify trap primers are installed and operational on all floor drains
Confirm water hammer arresters (J.R. Smith Fig. 5005) are installed at all fixture groups
Complete commissioning report with deficiency punch list and submit to commissioning authority

explanation: LEED enhanced commissioning requires systematic verification of every plumbing system component. The sequence moves from documentation review to fixture verification, firestopping, materials, safety interlocks, and finally the commissioning report. Deficiencies discovered during commissioning must be corrected and re-verified before the building receives LEED certification. This process catches the violations that routine inspections miss.
